Manual Timestamp Setting (Quick Fix, Not Recommended) If you want to avoid JPA Auditing in tests, manually set the timestamps: ```java OrderRepositoryTest.java Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/test/java/com/ecommerce/order/repository/OrderRepositoryTest.java @BeforeEach void setUp() { order = new Order(); order.setCustomerId("CUST123"); order.setOrderDate(LocalDateTime.now()); order.setStatus(OrderStatus.PENDING); order.setTotalAmount(new BigDecimal("59.98")); // ADDED: Manually set audit fields using reflection try { java.lang.reflect.Field createdAtField = Order.class.getDeclaredField("createdAt"); createdAtField.setAccessible(true); createdAtField.set(order, LocalDateTime.now()); java.lang.reflect.Field updatedAtField = Order.class.getDeclaredField("updatedAt"); updatedAtField.setAccessible(true); updatedAtField.set(order, LocalDateTime.now()); } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException("Failed to set audit fields", e); } OrderItem item = new OrderItem("ITEM123", 2, new BigDecimal("29.99")); order.addItem(item); entityManager.persist(order); entityManager.flush(); } ``` --- ## Verification Steps After applying the fix: ```bash # Clean and rebuild mvn clean install # Run only repository tests mvn test -Dtest=OrderRepositoryTest # Run all tests mvn test # Verify with verbose output mvn test -X -Dtest=OrderRepositoryTest ``` --- ## Explanation ### Why This Error Occurs 1. **JPA Auditing Annotations**: The [Order.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/model/entity/Order.java) entity uses `@CreatedDate` and `@LastModifiedDate` annotations (lines 42-47) 2. **Requires `@EnableJpaAuditing`**: These annotations only work when JPA Auditing is enabled 3. **`@DataJpaTest` doesn't enable it**: Slice tests load minimal context without auditing 4. **Database constraint**: The schema expects non-null values for `created_at` and `updated_at` ### How the Fix Works **Option 1 (Recommended):** - Adds `@EnableJpaAuditing` directly to the test class - Spring Data JPA automatically populates `@CreatedDate` and `@LastModifiedDate` fields - No code changes needed in the entity or test setup **Option 2 (Best for Multiple Tests):** - Creates a reusable test configuration with `@EnableJpaAuditing` - Can be imported by any test class that needs auditing - Provides an `AuditorAware` bean for user tracking (optional) **Option 3 (Not Recommended):** - Uses reflection to bypass JPA Auditing - Brittle and violates encapsulation - Only use as a last resort ### Trade-offs | Approach | Pros | Cons | |----------|------|------| | **Option 1** | ✅ Simple, one-line fix | ⚠️ Repeats annotation in each test class | | **Option 2** | ✅ Reusable, centralized | ⚠️ Requires additional config class | | **Option 3** | ✅ No auditing dependency | ❌ Fragile, uses reflection | **Recommended**: Use **Option 1** for quick fixes or **Option 2** if you have multiple test classes that need JPA Auditing. --- ## Additional Considerations ### If Using Flyway in Tests If you re-enable Flyway later, ensure your migration scripts create the audit columns: ```sql V1__create_order_tables.sql Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/db/migration/V1__create_order_tables.sql CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, order_date TIMESTAMP CURRENT_TIMESTAMP -- Audit field ); ``` ### Verify Entity Listener is Registered Ensure @Table(name = "orders") @EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class) // Required for @CreatedDate/@LastModifiedDate public class Order { // ... } ``` This is already present in your code, so no changes needed. "Asia/Calcutta"","selectedFiles":[],"agentId":null,"correlationId":null,"id":"7147437a-d07b-477c-948a-c520439a4b12","promptLibrary":null,"response":"# Fixing PostgreSQL TimeZone Error: "Asia/Calcutta" Invalid Value ## Root Cause Analysis The error occurs because: 1. **PostgreSQL doesn't recognize "Asia/Calcutta"** as a valid timezone identifier 2. **The timezone is likely set in your system environment** or database connection URL 3. **"Asia/Calcutta" is an obsolete timezone name** - PostgreSQL uses "Asia/Kolkata" (the modern IANA timezone identifier) --- ## Solution: Update Timezone Configuration ### Option 1: Update application.yml (Recommended) Create or update your `application.yml` to explicitly set the timezone: ```yaml application.yml Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.yml spring: application: name: order-service # Database Configuration datasource: url: ${DB_URL:jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/orderdb?TimeZone=Asia/Kolkata} username: ${DB_USERNAME:postgres} password: ${DB_PASSWORD:password} driver-class-name: org.postgresql.Driver # HikariCP Configuration hikari: pool-name: OrderServiceHikariPool maximum-pool-size: 10 minimum-idle: 5 connection-timeout: 30000 idle-timeout: 600000 max-lifetime: 1800000 auto-commit: true connection-test-query: SELECT 1 # ADDED: Explicitly set timezone for connections data-source-properties: serverTimezone: Asia/Kolkata # JPA Configuration jpa: database-platform: org.hibernate.dialect.PostgreSQLDialect hibernate: ddl-auto: validate show-sql: false properties: hibernate: format_sql: true jdbc: time_zone: Asia/Kolkata # ADDED: Set Hibernate timezone # Flyway Configuration flyway: enabled: true baseline-on-migrate: true locations: classpath:db/migration validate-on-migrate: true # Server Configuration server: port: ${SERVER_PORT:8081} # Logging Configuration logging: level: com.ecommerce.order: INFO org.springframework.web: INFO ``` --- ### Option 2: Update Environment Variables If you're using environment variables, update your `.env` file or system environment: ```properties .env Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/.env # Database Configuration - UPDATED timezone parameter DB_URL=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/orderdb?TimeZone=Asia/Kolkata DB_USERNAME=postgres DB_PASSWORD=your_password # Kafka Configuration KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS=localhost:9092 # JWT Configuration JWT_ISSUER_URI=http://localhost:8080/auth/realms/ecommerce # Server Configuration SERVER_PORT=8081 ``` --- ### Option 3: Update PostgreSQL Server Configuration If you have access to PostgreSQL server configuration, update `postgresql.conf`: ```conf postgresql.conf # Set the default timezone timezone = 'Asia/Kolkata' # Or use UTC for consistency across environments (recommended for production) # timezone = 'UTC' ``` Then restart PostgreSQL: ```bash # Linux/Mac sudo systemctl restart postgresql # Windows net stop postgresql-x64-14 net start postgresql-x64-14 # Docker docker restart postgres-container ``` --- ### Option 4: Use UTC (Production Best Practice) For production environments, it's recommended to use UTC to avoid timezone-related issues: ```yaml application.yml Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.yml spring: datasource: url: ${DB_URL:jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/orderdb?TimeZone=UTC} hikari: data-source-properties: serverTimezone: UTC jpa: properties: hibernate: jdbc: time_zone: UTC ``` Update your application to handle timezone conversions in the presentation layer: ```java OrderServiceImpl.java Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/service/OrderServiceImpl.java import java.time.ZoneId; import java.time.ZonedDateTime; // When returning timestamps to clients private OrderDTO mapToDTO(Order order) { OrderDTO dto = new OrderDTO(); dto.setOrderId(order.getOrderId()); dto.setCustomerId(order.getCustomerId()); dto.setStatus(order.getStatus()); dto.setTotalAmount(order.getTotalAmount()); // Convert UTC to user's timezone if needed dto.setOrderDate(order.getOrderDate()); // Store as UTC in DB // For items dto.setItems(order.getItems().stream() .map(this::mapItemToDTO) .collect(Collectors.toList())); return dto; } ``` --- ## Verification Steps After applying the fix: ```bash # 1. Clean and rebuild mvn clean install # 2. Check if the application starts successfully mvn spring-boot:run # 3. Verify database connection # Check application logs for successful connection # Look for: "HikariPool-1 - Start completed" # 4. Test with PostgreSQL client psql -h localhost -U postgres -d orderdb -c "SHOW timezone;" ``` --- ## Additional Fixes for [DatabaseConfig.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/config/DatabaseConfig.java) Since you have a custom `DatabaseConfig`, ensure it's not interfering: ```java DatabaseConfig.java Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/config/DatabaseConfig.java package com.ecommerce.order.config; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration; /** * Database configuration for Order Service. * Migrates from legacy H2 in-memory to production PostgreSQL. * * Note: HikariCP is auto-configured by Spring Boot via application properties. * Timezone configuration is handled in application.yml */ @Configuration public class DatabaseConfig { // Spring Boot auto-configures HikariCP DataSource // Configuration is externalized to application.yml // No custom DataSource bean needed } ``` --- ## Understanding Timezone Identifiers | Obsolete Name | Modern IANA Name | Notes | |---------------|------------------|-------| | `Asia/Calcutta` | `Asia/Kolkata` | City renamed in 2001 | | `Asia/Saigon` | `Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh` | City renamed | | `America/Buenos_Aires` | `America/Argentina/Buenos_Aires` | Region-specific | PostgreSQL uses the **IANA Time Zone Database**, which doesn't include obsolete names. --- ## Explanation ### Why This Error Occurs 1. **System Timezone**: Your system or JVM is set to use "Asia/Calcutta" 2. **JDBC Driver**: The PostgreSQL JDBC driver passes this timezone to the database 3. **PostgreSQL Validation**: PostgreSQL validates the timezone against its IANA database and rejects "Asia/Calcutta" 4. **Flyway Initialization**: The error occurs during Flyway's database connection attempt, before the application fully starts ### How the Fix Works **Option 1 (Recommended):** - Explicitly sets `TimeZone=Asia/Kolkata` in the JDBC URL - Configures HikariCP's `serverTimezone` property - Sets Hibernate's `time_zone` to ensure consistency - Overrides any system-level timezone settings **Option 2:** - Updates environment variables to use the correct timezone - Allows different timezones per environment (dev, staging, prod) **Option 3:** - Changes the PostgreSQL server's default timezone - Affects all databases on that server - Requires server restart **Option 4 (Production Best Practice):** - Uses UTC for all database operations - Eliminates timezone-related bugs - Handles timezone conversion in the application layer ### Trade-offs | Approach | Pros | Cons | |----------|------|------| | **Option 1** | ✅ Application-level control
✅ No server changes | ⚠️ Must configure in all environments | | **Option 2** | ✅ Environment-specific
✅ Easy to change | ⚠️ Requires env var management | | **Option 3** | ✅ Server-wide consistency | ❌ Requires server access
❌ Affects all databases | | **Option 4** | ✅ Production-ready
✅ No timezone bugs | ⚠️ Requires app-level conversion | --- ## Recommended Approach **For Development:** Use **Option 1** with `Asia/Kolkata` **For Production:** Use **Option 4** with `UTC` and handle timezone conversions in your DTOs/controllers This ensures: - ✅ No timezone-related data corruption - ✅ Consistent behavior across global deployments - ✅ Easy debugging and log correlation - ✅ Compliance with international standards Sources-Repos/Files: - Selected context","userText":"@Workspace getting below error while starting application Exception encountered during context initialization - Try to apply fix in Test class xception encountered during context initialization - cancelling refresh attempt: org.springframework.beans.factory.BeanCreationException: Error creating bean with name 'jpaAuditingHandler': Cannot resolve reference to bean 'jpaMappingContext' while setting constructor argument","selectedFiles":[],"agentId":null,"correlationId":null,"id":"56593db0-aa3f-464b-8497-f932f15bbdf9","promptLibrary":null,"response":"# Fixing JPA Auditing Bean Creation Error in Test Class ## Quick Fix Summary - **Add `@EnableJpaAuditing`** annotation to the test class to enable JPA auditing in the test context - **Alternative**: Create a separate test configuration class to avoid repeating the annotation - **Root cause**: `@WebMvcTest` doesn't load JPA auditing infrastructure by default --- ## Solution: Update OrderControllerTest.java Apply this fix directly in your test class: ```java OrderControllerTest.java Downloads\java legacy migration\opus\order-service\src\test\java\com\ecommerce\order\controller\OrderControllerTest.java package com.ecommerce.order.controller; import com.ecommerce.order.constants.OrderStatus; import com.ecommerce.order.model.dto.*; import com.ecommerce.order.service.OrderService; import com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.ObjectMapper; import org.junit.jupiter.api.BeforeEach; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired; import org.springframework.boot.test.autoconfigure.web.servlet.WebMvcTest; import org.springframework.boot.test.mock.mockito.MockBean; import org.springframework.context.annotation.ComponentScan; import org.springframework.context.annotation.FilterType; import org.springframework.data.jpa.repository.config.EnableJpaAuditing; import org.springframework.http.MediaType; import org.springframework.security.test.context.support.WithMockUser; import org.springframework.test.web.servlet.MockMvc; import java.math.BigDecimal; import java.time.LocalDateTime; import java.util.Arrays; import static org.mockito.ArgumentMatchers.any; import static org.mockito.ArgumentMatchers.eq; import static org.mockito.Mockito.when; import static org.springframework.security.test.web.servlet.request.SecurityMockMvcRequestPostProcessors.csrf; import static org.springframework.test.web.servlet.request.MockMvcRequestBuilders.*; import static org.springframework.test.web.servlet.result.MockMvcResultMatchers.*; /** * Unit tests for OrderController. */ @WebMvcTest(OrderController.class) @EnableJpaAuditing // ADDED: Enable JPA Auditing for test context class OrderControllerTest { @Autowired private MockMvc mockMvc; @Autowired private ObjectMapper objectMapper; @MockBean private OrderService orderService; private CreateOrderRequest createOrderRequest; private CreateOrderResponse createOrderResponse; private OrderDTO orderDTO; @BeforeEach void setUp() { // Setup test data OrderItemDTO item = new OrderItemDTO("ITEM123", 2, new BigDecimal("29.99")); createOrderRequest = new CreateOrderRequest(); createOrderRequest.setCustomerId("CUST123"); createOrderRequest.setItems(Arrays.asList(item)); createOrderResponse = new CreateOrderResponse( 1L, "CUST123", OrderStatus.PENDING, new BigDecimal("59.98"), LocalDateTime.now() ); orderDTO = new OrderDTO(); orderDTO.setOrderId(1L); orderDTO.setCustomerId("CUST123"); orderDTO.setStatus(OrderStatus.PENDING); orderDTO.setTotalAmount(new BigDecimal("59.98")); orderDTO.setOrderDate(LocalDateTime.now()); orderDTO.setItems(Arrays.asList(item)); } @Test @WithMockUser(roles = "CUSTOMER") void testCreateOrder_Success() throws Exception { when(orderService.createOrder(any(CreateOrderRequest.class))) .thenReturn(createOrderResponse); mockMvc.perform(post("/api/orders") .with(csrf()) .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON) .content(objectMapper.writeValueAsString(createOrderRequest))) .andExpect(status().isCreated()) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.orderId").value(1L)) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.customerId").value("CUST123")) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.status").value("PENDING")); } @Test @WithMockUser(roles = "CUSTOMER") void testGetOrderById_Success() throws Exception { when(orderService.getOrderById(1L)).thenReturn(orderDTO); mockMvc.perform(get("/api/orders/1")) .andExpect(status().isOk()) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.orderId").value(1L)) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.customerId").value("CUST123")); } @Test @WithMockUser(roles = "ADMIN") void testUpdateOrderStatus_Success() throws Exception { orderDTO.setStatus(OrderStatus.SHIPPED); when(orderService.updateOrderStatus(eq(1L), eq(OrderStatus.SHIPPED))) .thenReturn(orderDTO); UpdateOrderStatusRequest request = new UpdateOrderStatusRequest(OrderStatus.SHIPPED); mockMvc.perform(put("/api/orders/1/status") .with(csrf()) .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON) .content(objectMapper.writeValueAsString(request))) .andExpect(status().isOk()) .andExpect(jsonPath("$.status").value("SHIPPED")); } @Test void testCreateOrder_Unauthorized() throws Exception { mockMvc.perform(post("/api/orders") .with(csrf()) .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON) .content(objectMapper.writeValueAsString(createOrderRequest))) .andExpect(status().isUnauthorized()); } } ``` --- ## Alternative Solution: Create Reusable Test Configuration If you have multiple test classes that need JPA auditing, create a shared configuration: ```java TestJpaConfig.java Downloads\java legacy migration\opus\order-service\src\test\java\com\ecommerce\order\config\TestJpaConfig.java package com.ecommerce.order.config; import org.springframework.boot.test.context.TestConfiguration; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean; import org.springframework.data.domain.AuditorAware; import org.springframework.data.jpa.repository.config.EnableJpaAuditing; import java.util.Optional; /** * Test configuration for JPA Auditing. * Provides minimal setup for audit fields in tests. */ @TestConfiguration @EnableJpaAuditing public class TestJpaConfig { @Bean public AuditorAware auditorProvider() { // Return a fixed auditor for tests return () -> Optional.of("test-user"); } } ``` Then import it in your test: ```java OrderControllerTest.java Downloads\java legacy migration\opus\order-service\src\test\java\com\ecommerce\order\controller\OrderControllerTest.java @WebMvcTest(OrderController.class) @Import(TestJpaConfig.class) // ADDED: Import test configuration class OrderControllerTest { // ... rest of the test code } ``` --- ## Verification Steps After applying the fix: ```bash # Clean and rebuild mvn clean install # Run only the failing test mvn test -Dtest=OrderControllerTest # Run all tests mvn test ``` --- ## Explanation ### Why This Error Occurs 1. **`@WebMvcTest` is a slice test** - It only loads web layer components (controllers, filters, etc.) and doesn't load JPA infrastructure 2. **JPA Auditing requires `jpaMappingContext`** - This bean is created by JPA auto-configuration, which isn't loaded in `@WebMvcTest` 3. **`@EnableJpaAuditing` in main application** - Your main application class has this annotation, but the test context doesn't inherit it 4. **Bean creation fails** - Spring tries to create `jpaAuditingHandler` but can't find its dependency `jpaMappingContext` ### How the Fix Works **Option 1 (Quick Fix):** - Adding `@EnableJpaAuditing` to the test class enables JPA auditing infrastructure in the test context - Spring Boot auto-configures the necessary beans (`jpaMappingContext`, `jpaAuditingHandler`) - The test runs successfully without needing full JPA repository scanning **Option 2 (Reusable):** - Creates a `@TestConfiguration` class that can be imported by multiple tests - Provides an `AuditorAware` bean for user tracking (optional but recommended) - Keeps test classes clean and follows DRY principle ### Trade-offs | Approach | Pros | Cons | |----------|------|------| | **Option 1** | ✅ Simple one-line fix
✅ No additional files | ⚠️ Repeats annotation in each test class | | **Option 2** | ✅ Reusable across tests
✅ Centralized configuration | ⚠️ Requires additional config file | **Recommended**: Use **Option 1** for quick fixes or **Option 2** if you have multiple test classes that need JPA auditing. because **Hibernate is attempting to alter the `customer_id` column type in an existing PostgreSQL database**, but PostgreSQL cannot automatically cast the existing data type (likely `VARCHAR` or `TEXT` from legacy system) to `BIGINT`. The database requires an explicit `USING` clause to specify how to perform the conversion. ## Root Cause 1. **Schema Migration Conflict**: The `Order` entity (line 28 in [Order.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/model/entity/Order.java)) defines `customerId` as `Long`, but the existing database table has `customer_id` as a different type (likely `VARCHAR`/`TEXT` from the legacy system) 2. **Hibernate Auto-DDL**: Hibernate's schema update mechanism is trying to alter the column type but PostgreSQL rejects the automatic cast 3. **Missing Migration Strategy**: The migration from legacy H2 to PostgreSQL lacks proper database migration scripts ## Solution Options ### Option 1: Use Flyway/Liquibase Migration (Recommended) Create a proper migration script to handle the data type conversion: ```sql V1__alter_customer_id_type.sql Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/db/migration/V1__alter_customer_id_type.sql -- Migration script to convert customer_id from VARCHAR/TEXT to BIGINT -- Step 1: Add a temporary column ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN customer_id_temp BIGINT; -- Step 2: Copy and cast data (handle nulls and invalid data) UPDATE orders SET customer_id_temp = CASE WHEN customer_id ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN customer_id::BIGINT ELSE NULL END; -- Step 3: Drop old column ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN customer_id; -- Step 4: Rename temp column ALTER TABLE orders RENAME COLUMN customer_id_temp TO customer_id; -- Step 5: Add NOT NULL constraint ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN customer_id SET NOT NULL; ``` **Add Flyway dependency** to `pom.xml`: ```xml pom.xml Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/pom.xml org.flywaydb flyway-core org.flywaydb flyway-database-postgresql ``` **Update application properties**: ```properties application.properties Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.properties # Disable Hibernate auto-DDL spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate # Enable Flyway spring.flyway.enabled=true spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration ``` ### Option 2: Manual Database Fix (Quick Fix) If you need immediate resolution and can afford downtime: ```sql -- Execute directly on PostgreSQL database ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN customer_id TYPE BIGINT USING customer_id::BIGINT; ``` Then update your application properties: ```properties application.properties Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.properties # Change from 'update' to 'validate' to prevent Hibernate from altering schema spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate ``` ### Option 3: Drop and Recreate (Development Only) **WARNING: This will delete all data** ```sql -- Only for development/testing environments DROP TABLE IF EXISTS order_item CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders CASCADE; ``` Then set: ```properties application.properties Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.properties spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create ``` ## Recommended Fix Implementation **Step 1**: Update [DatabaseConfig.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/config/DatabaseConfig.java) to disable Hibernate DDL: ```java Java::DatabaseConfig.java::Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/config/DatabaseConfig.java package com.ecommerce.order.config; import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Value; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration; import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.flyway.FlywayMigrationStrategy; /** * Database configuration for Order Service. * Migrates from legacy H2 in-memory to production PostgreSQL. */ @Configuration public class DatabaseConfig { @Value("${spring.datasource.url}") private String jdbcUrl; @Value("${spring.datasource.username}") private String username; @Value("${spring.datasource.password}") private String password; /** * Flyway migration strategy for handling baseline */ @Bean public FlywayMigrationStrategy flywayMigrationStrategy() { return flyway -> { // Repair if needed flyway.repair(); // Migrate flyway.migrate(); }; } } ``` **Step 2**: Create the migration script as shown in Option 1 **Step 3**: Update application.properties: ```properties application.properties Downloads/java legacy migration/opus/order-service/src/main/resources/application.properties # Database Configuration spring.datasource.url=${POSTGRES_URL:jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/orderdb} spring.datasource.username=${POSTGRES_USER:postgres} spring.datasource.password=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:postgres} spring.datasource.driver-class-name=org.postgresql.Driver # JPA/Hibernate Configuration spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate spring.jpa.show-sql=true spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.dialect=org.hibernate.dialect.PostgreSQLDialect spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.format_sql=true # Flyway Configuration spring.flyway.enabled=true spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true spring.flyway.baseline-version=0 spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration spring.flyway.validate-on-migrate=true ``` ## Explanation **Why this happened:** - The legacy system likely stored `customerId` as a string type - Your new JPA entity defines it as `Long` (line 28 in [Order.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/main/java/com/ecommerce/order/model/entity/Order.java)) - PostgreSQL requires explicit casting instructions when converting incompatible types **Why Flyway is recommended:** - **Version Control**: Database schema changes are tracked and versioned - **Repeatability**: Migrations can be replayed across environments - **Safety**: Validates schema state before applying changes - **Rollback Support**: Can baseline and repair migrations **Trade-offs:** - Option 1 (Flyway): More setup but production-ready and maintainable - Option 2 (Manual): Quick but doesn't solve the underlying migration strategy gap - Option 3 (Drop/Recreate): Only suitable for development with no data preservation needs The test in [OrderRepositoryTest.java](Downloads/java%20legacy%20migration/opus/order-service/src/test/java/com/ecommerce/order/repository/OrderRepositoryTest.java) already disables Flyway (line 24), which is correct for unit tests using in-memory databases.
